selection_summary: Candidate diagnosis_20260421_155253:dir_01 closed via pass_loss after selection.

candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 128x128x32 Staged Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest measured-stall hypothesis to test because the alternative kernel body is already in-tree.
It keeps the same hot-band CTA shape while directly changing the per-stage wait and barrier cadence.
candidates.hypothesis: The current default hot-band kernel is paying too much synchronization and wait overhead per 16-wide K tile. The existing 128x128x32 staged sibling already consumes two K tiles per stage and should reduce barrier and long-scoreboard pressure in the dominant hot-band region without changing the overall 128x128 CTA footprint.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: synchronization_barrier_issue and long_scoreboard latency in the current hot-band 128x128 PTX microkernel
candidates.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the kernel already exists, but the alternate staging schedule may trade barrier relief for higher shared-memory traffic or a small register increase.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime does not improve by at least 0.3 ms or if barrier and long-scoreboard stalls stay flat within measurement noise.
expected_local_changes: Swap the default fixed-shape hot-band launch from the PTX microkernel path to the existing 128x128x32 staged kernel.
Keep the residual 64x384 row-band and 64x96 tail unchanged for the first measurement.

candidates.direction_name: Collapse The PTX Microkernel Wait-And-Sync Cadence
ranking_notes: The custom-vs-cuBLAS gap is dominated by synchronization and scoreboard stalls, so this has the clearest direct path to the remaining ~1.9 ms gap.
It ranks behind dir_01 only because the existing staged sibling provides a cheaper first measurement.
candidates.hypothesis: The current PTX hot-band loop calls cp.async wait plus a CTA sync after every K tile, which matches the observed 5.49% barrier and 7.19% long-scoreboard cost. Porting a pairwise or two-K stage advance pattern, similar to the peeled 64x384 helper, should reduce the synchronization tax while keeping the current microkernel math path.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: per-tile cp.async wait plus CTA barrier cadence in the PTX hot-band microkernel
candidates.risk: Moderate-to-high risk: this edits the PTX hot-band steady-state pipeline directly, so a small synchronization mistake can break correctness or regress tensor feed rate.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the edited loop reduces barrier stalls but causes correctness failures, or if runtime regresses by more than 0.5 ms after the first buildable attempt.
expected_local_changes: Refactor the PTX microkernel steady-state loop so future-stage refill happens in a pairwise cadence instead of waiting and syncing after every single K tile.
Reuse the existing peeled-row-band stage-advance helper pattern where possible to keep the sync semantics auditable.

candidates.direction_name: Push More Hot-Band Rows Into The 64x384 Peeled Path
ranking_notes: The 64x384 family is supported by old fixed-tile measurements, but the current profile still points first at the 128x128 PTX hotspot, so this ranks third.
This is still a good fallback if the staged 128x128 sibling and PTX wait-cadence rewrite both fail to close the gap.
candidates.hypothesis: The historical fixed-main-tile sweep already found 64x384 to be the strongest hot-band width family. Rebalancing the fixed split so fewer rows use the 128x128 PTX hotspot and more rows use the peeled 64x384 path may reduce CTA scheduling and synchronization overhead without rewriting the microkernel itself.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: hot-band decomposition choice is leaving too much work on the slower 128x128 PTX hotspot instead of the best historical 384-wide family
candidates.risk: Moderate risk: the old tile sweep favored 384 width, but expanding that path changes the row split and could expose new register or launch overhead outside the tiny residual band it currently covers.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the rebalance improves the profiled kernel metrics but total runtime does not move, which would imply the split change only shifted work rather than removing the dominant bottleneck.
expected_local_changes: Retune kFixedPivotHotRows so a larger slice of the hot band launches through the peeled 64x384 path.
Keep the 64x96 tail kernel unchanged while measuring whether the split rebalance alone helps.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Accepted Base And Expand The 64x384 Row Band
ranking_notes: Round 1 ruled out the staged 128x128 branch, so the next move should start from the accepted PTX baseline rather than stack another edit on the regressed head.
The 64x384 family is the only alternative with direct measured support in the repo state.
candidates.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band launch is still the best measured baseline, but too much of the fixed shape remains on the 128x128 hotspot. Restoring that baseline and reducing kFixedPivotHotRows should move more work into the historically stronger 64x384 peeled path, cutting aggregate barrier exposure without reintroducing the staged-128x128 regression.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: too much of the fixed hot band is assigned to the 128x128 PTX hotspot instead of the stronger 64x384 family
candidates.risk: Moderate risk: the historical 64x384 sweep predates the newest PTX hot-band refinements, so moving too many rows may simply shift the bottleneck instead of shrinking it.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime returns to the accepted base but does not improve it by at least 0.2 ms, or if barrier stalls fall while total runtime stays flat.
expected_local_changes: Restore the default fixed-shape hot-band launch from the regressed 128x128x32 staged sibling back to the accepted PTX hot-band kernel.
Lower kFixedPivotHotRows so more hot-band rows run through the 64x384 peeled path while keeping the 64x96 tail unchanged.

candidates.direction_name: Retune PTX Hot-Band Grouped-Row Traversal
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest clean experiment on top of the accepted PTX path.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the historical sweep gives stronger support to the 64x384 family than to any specific grouped-row value.
candidates.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band kernel may be paying unnecessary launch-order and cache-reuse penalties from the current four-row grouped traversal. Retuning kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows changes the block issue pattern without reopening the heavy staged-kernel path.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: launch-order and cache/locality inefficiency inside the accepted PTX hot-band traversal
candidates.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the edit is small, but the effect can easily be noise-sized if grouped traversal is not actually the main issue.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change is within measurement noise after one buildable attempt.
expected_local_changes: Keep the accepted PTX hot-band launch in place.
Retune kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows and measure whether CTA issue order and locality improve.

candidates.direction_name: Swap To The Single-K 128x128 Non-Microkernel Sibling
ranking_notes: This is a controlled kernel-variant swap with less shared-memory expansion than the staged 128x128x32 path.
It ranks third because there is no direct in-repo measurement yet showing the regular 128x128 sibling beats the accepted PTX microkernel.
candidates.hypothesis: The existing single-K 128x128 sibling keeps the same stage depth as the accepted PTX path but uses the regular accumulate helper instead of the microkernel-specific ordering. That may trim scheduler or register overhead without paying the staged-128x128x32 cost.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate/store scheduling inside the accepted 128x128 hot-band path
candidates.risk: Moderate risk: this is a variant swap without direct performance evidence on the current refactor branch, so it can regress just as easily as it improves.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if registers climb or active-warps fall relative to the accepted PTX base without a runtime improvement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX baseline if needed, then replace the default hot-band launch with the single-K non-microkernel 128x128 sibling.
Keep the residual 64x384 path and 64x96 tail unchanged for the first comparison.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Accepted Base And Increase PTX Grouped-Row Depth
ranking_notes: The accepted PTX base still beats every attempted variant in this refactor loop, so the next move should be a small perturbation on that path.
Grouped-row depth is the cleanest way to change CTA issue order and B reuse without touching the K-stage structure.
candidates.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band kernel is still the fastest measured family, but its grouped-row traversal may not be feeding B-tile reuse aggressively enough. Restoring the accepted base and increasing kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 should keep more adjacent row tiles on the same hot-band B tile before advancing columns, which may reduce scoreboard waste without reopening the failed 64x384 expansion.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: launch-order and B-tile reuse inefficiency inside the accepted PTX hot-band traversal
candidates.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the change is isolated, but a larger grouped-row depth can easily help B reuse while hurting A locality.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change stays within noise or if long-scoreboard stalls rise further after the first measurement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band baseline by undoing the round-2 pivot-row rebalance.
Increase kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 while keeping the default PTX hot-band launch and 64x96 tail intact.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Accepted Base And Swap To The Single-K 128x128 Sibling
ranking_notes: This is the most contained kernel-variant swap that stays on the accepted row split.
It ranks behind grouped-row retuning because the launch-order experiment is cheaper and more directly tied to the current scoreboard signal.
candidates.hypothesis: The existing single-K 128x128 sibling preserves the accepted row split and one-K staging depth while using the regular accumulate ordering instead of the microkernel-specific path. That may improve scoreboard behavior without paying the staged-128x128x32 penalty or the 64x384 row-band penalty.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is contributing to scoreboard overhead on the accepted hot-band split
candidates.risk: Moderate risk: the variant is in-tree but still lacks a direct measurement on the accepted row split in this refactor loop.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses again while register pressure and active-warps do not materially improve.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band baseline row split.
Replace the default hot-band launch with the existing single-K 128x128 non-microkernel sibling.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Accepted Base And Tighten PTX Launch Bounds
ranking_notes: This is still worth keeping because the PTX path remains latency-limited.
It ranks third because the cuBLAS ceiling does not point to occupancy as the main missing ingredient.
candidates.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hotspot still sits around 200 registers per thread with occupancy capped at two blocks per SM. Tightening __launch_bounds__ to target three blocks per SM may trim enough register pressure to change latency hiding, provided the compiler does not spill heavily.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: register pressure and low CTA residency on the accepted PTX hot-band path
candidates.risk: High risk: forcing tighter launch bounds can introduce spills or a weaker instruction schedule, and the cuBLAS reference suggests occupancy alone is not the full answer.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop immediately if build output or NCU shows spills, or if runtime regresses without reducing register pressure.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band baseline row split.
Retune the PTX microkernel launch-bounds annotation and leave the rest of the launch topology unchanged for the first measurement.

candidates.direction_name: Increase PTX Grouped-Row Depth On The Clean Baseline
ranking_notes: This is the fastest clean experiment to run on the accepted PTX hot-band baseline.
It keeps the search on the same family that currently produces the best custom runtime.
candidates.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band baseline may still be wasting issue slots because each hot-band B tile is only reused across four adjacent row tiles before the traversal advances columns. Increasing kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 should keep each B tile live across more rows, potentially reducing scoreboard pressure without changing the row split or K-stage structure.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: launch-order and B-tile reuse inefficiency inside the accepted PTX hot-band traversal
candidates.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the edit is isolated, but deeper row grouping can help B reuse while hurting A locality or cache balance.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change stays within noise or if long-scoreboard stalls rise after the first clean measurement.
expected_local_changes: Keep the accepted PTX hot-band row split and default launch intact.
Increase kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 and remeasure on the same fixed benchmark.

candidates.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is contributing to scoreboard overhead on the accepted hot-band split

candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses while register pressure and active-warps do not materially improve.
expected_local_changes: Keep the accepted PTX hot-band row split.

candidates.direction_name: Try A Shallower PTX Grouped-Row Setting
ranking_notes: This stays in reserve as the opposite-direction launch-order test after the 8-row setting failed.
It ranks third because the non-microkernel sibling is now the more direct scoreboard experiment.
candidates.hypothesis: The failed 8-row grouping suggests the PTX hot-band path may prefer tighter A locality over deeper B reuse. Dropping kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 2 tests the opposite launch-order direction without changing the row split or K-stage structure.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: current grouped-row traversal may still be mismatched to A/B locality balance on the accepted PTX hot-band path
candidates.risk: Moderate risk: this is the same family that just failed in the opposite direction, so the upside may be small.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change is within noise or if scoreboard stalls do not improve after one measurement.

candidates.direction_name: Trim Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
ranking_notes: This is the right 128x128 follow-on family after recovery, but not the right thing to auto-select while the live surface is still sitting at 28.20 ms.
Its value is that it respects the lesson from round 30: barrier amortization must not come from doubling the hot-band shared-memory footprint.
Rehydrated from master heuristic history to widen family coverage on the clean refactor branch.
Implementation should start from the clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4 rather than replaying the original historical branch state.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 29 and round 30 together now fence off both obvious extremes on the 128x128 occupancy-first path. The launch-bounds probe reduced registers successfully but doubled barrier cost. The two-K-stage kernel reduced per-stage synchronization pressure but doubled shared-memory footprint enough to destroy the occupancy gain. That leaves one bounded but still aggressive follow-up family: keep the smaller 22,016 B shared-memory microkernel footprint from round 29 and attack the barrier cost more surgically through wait/sync cadence or export scheduling rather than by introducing a second full K-tile buffer pair.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence inside the single-K 128x128 PTX microkernel while preserving the lower shared-memory footprint.
candidates.risk: High. This is a more manual PTX-microkernel surgery path than the exact restore.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the edit pushes shared memory above the round-29 footprint again or if it fails to lower barrier stall materially from the round-29 10.97% level.
candidates.notes: Seeded on refactor branch from master diagnosis diagnosis_20260421_013500 / run 20260421_013416_bf16_gemm_v1_17032e6; rebound to clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4.

candidates.direction_name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
ranking_notes: This family is kept above the exact restore because it is one of the only branches that ever moved the machine state off the 16.6%-warps plateau.
It is ranked second rather than first because the code motion needed to keep the low-register budget without reviving ownership bugs is substantially larger than the round-29 launch-bounds probe.
Rehydrated from master heuristic history to widen family coverage on the clean refactor branch.
Implementation should start from the clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4 rather than replaying the original historical branch state.
candidates.hypothesis: The broken half-panel branch is not a candidate to replay as-is, but it did prove one important structural fact: the repo can reach about 92-93 registers per thread, about 32.9% active warps, and very low long-scoreboard stalls on a 256x128 hot-band geometry. The correct 256x128 pivot branch, by contrast, stayed near 167 registers, 16.7% warps, and 30 ms. The aggressive but still coherent next family is to start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot ownership path and selectively transplant the low-register staging and reuse ideas that made the half-panel branch interesting, while refusing to reintroduce the half-panel writer split that broke correctness.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and steady-state staging efficiency inside the 256x128 hot-band path, plus barrier overhead created by the old half-panel ownership split.
candidates.risk: High. This is the highest-ceiling family left in the repo, but it touches the same 256x128 control/export surface that already produced two correctness failures when ownership got too clever.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
candidates.stop_condition: Stop immediately if correctness fails again or if barrier stall stays above about 15% even after recovering low-register behavior; a fast-but-wrong 256x128 branch is not useful search progress.
candidates.notes: Seeded on refactor branch from master diagnosis diagnosis_20260421_012211 / run 20260421_012143_bf16_gemm_v1_803e749; rebound to clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4.

candidates.direction_name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest way to test whether the current gap versus the best measured run is coming from hot-loop control overhead instead of macro geometry.
It touches both active 128x128 hot-band variants without reopening the old restore-or-replay path.
Rehydrated from master heuristic history to widen family coverage on the clean refactor branch.
Implementation should start from the clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4 rather than replaying the original historical branch state.
candidates.hypothesis: Both active 128x128 hot-band kernels recompute warp-local A-row and B-column shared offsets inside the K steady-state loop even though those offsets are invariant for the entire CTA. Hoisting those offsets once per warp should shave integer address work and reduce control dilution on a path where tensor activity is only 48.37 percent while DRAM is far from saturated.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-pointer arithmetic and loop-carried control overhead in the 128x128 hot-band steady state are stealing issue slots from tensor work.
candidates.risk: Low. The change is local, preserves the same tile geometry, and only hoists invariant offset math.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation needs to change CTA geometry, cp.async stage count, or the dispatch map instead of remaining a local offset-hoist cleanup.
candidates.notes: Seeded on refactor branch from master diagnosis diagnosis_20260421_093739 / run 20260421_084952_bf16_gemm_v1_8b1af08; rebound to clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4.

candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: legacy diagnosis payload was backfilled into the search schema; keep this candidate as a lower-priority alternative until newer search-native scoring exists
Rehydrated from master heuristic history to widen family coverage on the clean refactor branch.
Implementation should start from the clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4 rather than replaying the original historical branch state.
candidates.hypothesis: With no extra human-review idea family queued, the third direction should be a materially different hot-band family rather than a return to stale cleanup work. The active recovered branch still launches the main hot band as a four-warp `128x128` CTA with `196` regs/thread, `22,016` B shared/block, register/shared occupancy caps of `2/2`, and only `16.64%` active warps. The in-tree `256x128` pivot kernel is the cleanest remaining way to test whether CTA geometry and block-count overhead, not just K16 cadence, are the limit on the first `6400 x 7680` rows. It halves the pivot-band CTA count while restoring an eight-warp CTA, which is materially different from both the active one-K branch and the PTX control variant.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band CTA geometry and block-count overhead in the current four-warp `128x128` path, rather than a pure copy-cadence issue.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The `256x128` pivot family already exists, but it has not been the accepted default on the restored surface, and a larger CTA can still lose if it trades CTA-count relief for a worse live-set or export footprint.
metrics_to_recheck: hot-band kernel name and grid size in `ncu_metrics.csv` to confirm the pivot CTA-count change
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the `256x128` pivot family cannot beat the current one-K `128x128` control run or if it reintroduces the kind of wide-family regression already seen when the stale `64x384` route was reopened.
candidates.notes: Seeded on refactor branch from master diagnosis diagnosis_20260420_200135 / run 20260420_200110_bf16_gemm_v1_e6fdb8b; rebound to clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4.

candidates.direction_name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest follow-on after the regular-sibling experiment because it preserves the same recovered register footprint and tests whether control overhead is the remaining gap.
It also matches the user request to inject a materially different family without reopening a stale negative branch immediately.
This is the least aligned with the new 256x128 tiling preference, but it is still the cheapest next exploit on the current live 128x128 surface.
candidates.hypothesis: The regular 128x128 sibling already showed that a small register reduction and a cleaner consume path can recover some of the grouped-row loss. The next cheapest win is to hoist warp-local A-row and B-column shared offsets out of the hot-band steady-state loops so the active 128x128 path spends fewer issue slots on invariant pointer math while keeping the same CTA geometry, stage depth, and shared-memory footprint.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-pointer arithmetic and loop-carried control overhead in the 128x128 hot-band steady state are still diluting tensor issue on a latency-limited path.
candidates.risk: Low. The change stays inside the active 128x128 hot-band loops and should not alter CTA geometry, staging depth, or ownership.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation needs to change CTA geometry, stage count, or dispatch order instead of remaining a local offset-hoist cleanup.
expected_local_changes: Precompute warp-local A and B shared offsets once per warp before the 128x128 steady-state loops.
Keep the current single-K staging and grouped_rows=4 traversal unchanged.

candidates.direction_name: Trim Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
ranking_notes: This keeps the highest-value PTX family alive without reopening the shared-memory blowup path that was already falsified.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the current live run suggests there is still cheaper control-overhead cleanup available on the active sibling.
This direction stays consistent with the human guidance on async copy, shared-memory reuse, and staging depth, but keeps the risk on the accepted PTX surface bounded.
candidates.hypothesis: The accepted clean base is still the PTX microkernel surface, and the current regular sibling only recovered part of the loss. If the low-risk exploit is not enough, the next best family is to go back to the PTX single-K surface and trim wait/sync cadence more surgically while preserving the 22,016 B shared-memory footprint, instead of repeating the two-K-stage experiment that destroyed occupancy.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence inside the single-K 128x128 PTX microkernel is still interrupting the steady-state issue flow even on the smaller shared-memory footprint.
candidates.risk: High. This is PTX-microkernel surgery on the accepted surface, so small schedule mistakes can erase the current stability or reintroduce barrier spikes.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory rises above the accepted 22,016 B footprint or if barrier stall does not improve materially versus the PTX accepted-base profile.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX microkernel launch surface if needed before editing the wait/sync cadence.
Retune the single-K consumer-barrier handoff without adding a second full K-tile shared buffer pair.

candidates.direction_name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
ranking_notes: This remains in the top three because it is one of the only branches in repo history that materially attacked the occupancy plateau instead of nudging stalls by tenths.
It ranks third because the correctness and implementation surface are both much larger than the two 128x128 options above it.
This is the direction that matches the new human guidance most directly because it pushes toward 256x128 tiling, stronger register reuse, and a deeper pipeline model.

The disciplined version is not to replay the broken branch, but to start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot ownership model and transplant only the low-register staging ideas that made the archived half-panel branch interesting.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and steady-state staging efficiency inside the 256x128 hot-band path are still capping residency and latency hiding on the current workload.

candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: This is the simplest direction that directly matches the new persistent human guidance around 256x128 tiling and 64x64 warp tiles.
It outranks dir_02 because it is already in-tree and therefore tests the geometry thesis without immediately paying the cost of a correctness-sensitive rewrite.
candidates.hypothesis: The 128x128 family has now delivered two incremental wins without moving achieved warps or occupancy limits. The cleanest next structural test is to route the default hot-band launch onto the existing 256x128 pivot kernel so the search directly tests the human-guided 256x128 / 64x64 tiling preference and a materially different CTA geometry on the accepted clean base.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Current four-warp 128x128 CTA geometry is capping residency and CTA-count efficiency on the hot-band region more than local pointer arithmetic is.
